title: Binary file handler ignores Page row visibility — only checks file ACL
description:
## Symptom

A .txt (or .pdf, .png, etc.) file uploaded via the API with `visibility: public` is correctly stored in the repo and indexed, but anonymous browser requests 404 unless a matching ACL grant exists in `.wikihub/acl`.

## Root cause

`app/routes/wiki.py` (binary-serving branch ~line 826) checks ACL via:
```python
file_vis = resolve_visibility(page_path, acl_rules)
if not is_owner and not can_read(page_path, acl_rules, user_name, file_vis):
    abort(404)
```

This uses **file-path ACL only**. The markdown branch checks the **Page row's visibility** as well. Page row visibility (set explicitly by `visibility: public` in the API call) is ignored for non-md files.

## Reproduction (verified)

1. Create wiki with default ACL (`* private`)
2. Upload a .txt: `POST /api/v1/wikis/USER/SLUG/pages {path: 'foo.txt', visibility: 'public'}` — succeeds
3. Anonymous browser GET `/@USER/SLUG/foo.txt` — 404
4. Add `foo.txt public` to `.wikihub/acl` — now 200

## Fix

In the binary handler, also accept the request if a matching Page row exists with public/public-edit visibility. Mirror the check in the markdown path.

## Related

- Closes loop with wikihub-uevw (this work). The .txt rendering fix landed but exposed this preexisting ACL inconsistency.
- Worked around for vision-convo wiki by adding `raw/** public` and `wiki/** public` ACL lines.

## Files

- `app/routes/wiki.py` ~826 (binary serving branch)
- `app/git_sync.py` `update_mirror_page` may also need updating to mirror non-md files based on Page row visibility

title: Redesign clone URL UI to match GitHub's pattern
description:
## Problem

The current clone UI is a plain "Copy clone URL" button in a dropdown. It copies a bare URL like `https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`. This is unfamiliar to users who expect GitHub's clone dialog pattern, and it doesn't communicate what you're actually getting.

## Design

Replace with a GitHub-style clone popover:

1. **Tabbed protocol selector**: HTTPS (default) | SSH (future) | CLI
2. **Pre-filled command** in a monospace input box:
   - HTTPS tab: `git clone https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.git`
   - CLI tab: `curl -L https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/rsi-wiki.zip -o rsi-wiki.zip`
3. **Copy button** (clipboard icon) next to the input
4. **Info box** below: subtle note explaining what you're cloning

## The Two-Repo Problem

WikiHub has two repos per wiki:
- `<slug>.git` — authoritative (owner-only read/write)
- `<slug>-public.git` — derived mirror (everyone else, read-only)

The git backend already handles this transparently — the same URL routes to different repos based on auth:
- Owner with API key → authoritative repo (can push)
- Anyone else → public mirror (read-only, no private pages)

**This is fine.** The URL is the same either way. But the UI should communicate:
- For non-owners: "This clones the public content. Private pages are excluded."
- For owners: "This clones your full wiki including private pages. Use your API key as password."

## Info Box Copy

Non-owner view:
> "Clones public pages only. Private pages are excluded from this view."

Owner view:
> "Use your API key as the password. This includes all pages."

## No push-back problem

Unlike GitHub forks, there's no expectation of pushing back to someone else's wiki via git. The clone is read-only for non-owners (the git backend rejects pushes from non-owners). If someone wants to contribute, they fork via the API/UI, which creates a separate wiki they own. So there's no confusion about "I cloned it but can't push" — it's the expected behavior and matches GitHub's model for repos you don't have write access to.
close_reason: Implemented GitHub-style clone command UI. Info box for public/private context deferred.

title: Return 404 instead of 403 for unauthorized private pages to prevent enumeration
File support roadmap: from image embeds to Google Drive mode
description:
Progressive file support beyond markdown-only browsing. The spec already covers storing binaries in git repos and rendering Obsidian ![[image.png]] embeds, but there's no serving route (images 404) and the sidebar/UI is markdown-only.

**Progressive stages (each unlocks the next):**

Stage 0 — Serve binaries from git repo (bug fix, not feature)
- Route to serve raw files from authoritative/public repo via git cat-file
- Content-type detection, cache headers
- Prerequisite for ![[image.png]] embeds to actually render

Stage 1 — Image attachments (core LLMWiki need)
- Upload images alongside markdown pages
- Inline image preview in editor
- Image gallery or attachment panel per page
- LLM wikis need diagrams, charts, screenshots — this is table stakes

Stage 2 — File browser
- Show all files in sidebar/tree, not just .md
- File type icons
- Non-markdown files visible but clearly secondary to wiki pages

Stage 3 — Non-markdown preview
- Inline PDF viewer, CSV table render, image lightbox
- Reference material browsable alongside wiki pages

Stage 4 — Google Drive mode (future, maybe separate product)
- Drag-drop any file type, bulk upload
- Full-text search across non-markdown files
- Folder-first browsing (vs page-first)
- This diverges from LLMWiki core mission — needs careful product thinking

**Key question:** How far past Stage 1 should we go before it stops being an LLMWiki and starts being a document hosting platform? The Karpathy pattern is markdown knowledge bases maintained by LLMs — images support that, but a Google Drive clone doesn't.

**ListHub contrast:** ListHub is deliberately text-only (flat markdown items, no binary support). WikiHub already went further architecturally (git repos can hold anything), but the serving/browsing layer is markdown-only. The gap is between what git stores and what the UI shows.

**Spec reference:** Binary storage is already specced (wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md, Data Architecture section). This epic covers the serving, browsing, and UX layers on top.

title: Wikipedia-style URLs: replace %20 with underscores
description:
## Context

WikiHub URLs currently use %20 for spaces in page names, making them ugly and hard to share:
`/@jacobcole/wiki/Tools%20%26%20Implementations`

## Research: How other platforms handle spaces

| Platform | Space handling | Example |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| **Wikipedia** | Underscores (`_`) | `/wiki/Machine_learning` |
| **Obsidian Publish** | Plus signs (`+`) | `/garden/My+Great+Note` |
| **GitHub wikis** | Hyphens (`-`) | `/wiki/My-Page` |
| **Most web apps** | `%20` (default) | `/page/My%20Page` |

## Obsidian Publish details
- $8-10/mo per site
- Plus signs for spaces, case-sensitive, no auto-slugification
- Supports `permalink` frontmatter override for custom slugs
- Password protection is all-or-nothing (no per-page ACL)
- No API, no git backing, no social features (fork/star)

## Recommendation: Wikipedia-style underscores

**Why underscores win:**
- Most readable: `The_Pattern` vs `The%20Pattern` vs `The+Pattern`
- Reversible: underscore ↔ space is lossless (filenames rarely have literal underscores)
- Battle-tested: Wikipedia has done this for 25 years, billions of URLs
- Obsidian-compatible: file stays `The Pattern.md` on disk, only URL changes
- Accept all forms: `The_Pattern`, `The%20Pattern`, and `The Pattern` should all resolve

**Implementation:**
- URL generation: `replace(" ", "_")` in all `_page_url()` and template helpers
- URL resolution: `replace("_", " ")` when looking up filenames
- Redirect: `%20` URLs → canonical underscore URLs (301)
- Edge case: files with literal underscores in names — rare, but need a policy (escape? accept ambiguity?)

**Bonus: permalink frontmatter support**
- Steal Obsidian Publish's `permalink` frontmatter field
- Let users set custom URL slugs per-page
- Useful for stable URLs that survive renames

## Ask Harrison
- Does underscore feel right, or would he prefer a different convention?
- Should we also lowercase URLs (case-insensitive matching)?
- How to handle the `&` in names like "Tools & Implementations" — underscore only handles spaces
close_reason: Implemented Wikipedia-style URLs: spaces in page names are now displayed as underscores in URLs (e.g., My_Great_Page instead of My%20Great%20Page). Added url_utils.py for path conversion, updated all route handlers to normalize underscore→space on lookup, added 301 redirects from %20 URLs to canonical underscore URLs, and updated all templates to use the page_url filter.

title: Curator Agent: inline AI sidebar for wiki curation and navigation
description:
## Vision

An inline AI sidebar on every wiki page — inspired by Neal Stephenson's Librarian (Snow Crash) and the Curator (Ready Player One). A conversational agent that knows who you are, what page you're on, and can take actions on wikis you have access to.

### Simplest use case
You're browsing someone's idea list. You say "promote this to the featured ideas list." The agent reads the current page, writes to the featured wiki (which is public-edit), done.

### Key references
- **Snow Crash Librarian** — already in WikiHub philosophy docs
- **Ready Player One Curator** — guides users through a vast archive
- **Noos project** — Jacob's existing coding-agent-backed knowledge graph (uses Claude Code under the hood)

---

## Architecture: Coding Agent with Filesystem Access (Community Standard)

### Research finding (2026-04-10)

The LLM Wiki ecosystem overwhelmingly uses **coding agents with direct filesystem access**, not API-mediated tool use:

| Pattern | Examples | Share |
|---------|----------|-------|
| **Coding agent (filesystem)** | SamurAIGPT (1.5k★), kfchou, Astro-Han, MehmetGoekce | ~80% |
| **MCP server (API-mediated)** | lucasastorian, sage-wiki | ~15% |
| **Desktop app** | nashsu | ~5% |

Karpathy's gist is literally a CLAUDE.md template — the agent opens a directory, reads the schema, and reads/writes markdown files directly. No API, no database, no custom tools.

### Why this matters for WikiHub

WikiHub's storage is already git repos of markdown files. The Curator doesn't need a custom tool-use layer — it can work on the files directly:

1. **Clone wiki repo** to a temp working directory
2. **Run coding agent** (Claude API with bash+file tools, or Claude Code subprocess) pointed at that directory
3. **Agent operates on markdown files natively** — the exact pattern the community uses
4. **Push changes back** via git push or WikiHub API
5. **Post-receive hook** syncs changes to DB (already exists)

This is simpler AND more capable than building custom API tools, because:
- Agent can do anything with the files (not limited to predefined tools)
- Matches how the entire community already works with LLM wikis
- CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md schema file is the standard discovery mechanism
- WikiHub's per-wiki AGENTS.md already exists and serves this purpose

### Execution model: Claude API with code execution tools

Use the Claude API (not a Claude Code subprocess) with these tools:
- `bash` — run shell commands (scoped to the temp working dir)
- `read_file` — read files in the working dir
- `write_file` — write files in the working dir
- `edit_file` — patch files

This gives coding-agent capabilities without process management complexity. The server:
1. Creates a temp dir per session
2. Clones the relevant wiki repo(s) into it
3. Runs Claude API calls with file/bash tools scoped to that dir
4. On agent actions that modify files, commits and pushes back to WikiHub
5. Cleans up temp dir when session ends

### Sandboxing (MVP vs Production)

| Approach | Isolation | Complexity | When |
|----------|-----------|------------|------|
| **Temp dir + chdir** | Process-level | Low | MVP — sufficient for trusted users |
| **Unix user per session** | User-level | Medium | v1.1 — if multi-tenant |
| **Docker per session** | Container-level | High | v2 — if running untrusted code |

For MVP, a temp directory with the agent's bash tool restricted to that path is adequate. The agent can only read/write files it was given access to.

### Multi-wiki operations

The "promote to featured" use case requires access to TWO wikis (source + destination). The temp dir can contain multiple cloned repos:

```
/tmp/curator-session-abc/
  jacobcole/ideas/          ← cloned from /@jacobcole/ideas
  wikihub/wikihub/          ← cloned from /@wikihub/wikihub (public-edit)
```

The agent's CLAUDE.md tells it the layout and what it can do.

---

## Agent Context (injected as system prompt / CLAUDE.md)

```
You are the Curator, WikiHub's AI librarian.

## Current session
- User: @{username}
- Page: /@{owner}/{wiki}/{page}
- Permissions: {read/write/admin}

## Working directory layout
- {owner}/{wiki}/          ← the wiki you're viewing
- wikihub/wikihub/         ← the main WikiHub wiki (public-edit)
- {other cloned wikis as needed}

## How to make changes
Edit files directly. Changes are auto-committed and pushed to WikiHub.
Follow the wiki's schema.md for structure rules.

## Available wikis for this user
{list of wikis the user can read/write}
```

---

## MVP Scope

### Backend
- `POST /api/v1/agent/chat` — SSE endpoint, requires auth
  - Input: `{message, conversation_id?, page_context?}`
  - Output: SSE stream of agent text + tool call results
  - Creates temp dir, clones relevant repos, runs Claude API with file tools
- Session management: temp dir per conversation, cleanup on timeout
- Git integration: auto-commit + push on file writes

### Frontend
- Slide-out panel on right side of page (does not replace left nav sidebar)
- Toggle button in page actions area (or floating button)
- Chat input at bottom, messages scroll up
- Tool calls shown inline (collapsed by default, expandable)
- "Curator is editing..." indicator when writing
- Desktop only for MVP; hidden on mobile

### System prompt
- Injected with user identity, current page, wiki structure
- References the wiki's own AGENTS.md/schema.md for rules
- Tells agent to work directly with files in the temp dir

---

## Chrome Extension (v2)

A personal Curator that works on any website:
- Sidebar Chrome extension (connects to Cortex project)
- On WikiHub pages: uses the built-in Curator API
- On other sites: personal agent mode (save to your wiki, annotate)
- If built-in sidebar AND extension both present:
  - Extension detects WikiHub, defers to built-in (or takes over — user pref)

---

## Immediate Next Steps
1. Make wikihub/wikihub wiki public-edit
2. Add /api/v1/agent/chat SSE endpoint
3. Implement temp-dir clone + file tools for Claude API
4. Build frontend slide-out panel
5. Test: "promote this idea to featured" end-to-end

## Files involved
- NEW: app/routes/agent_chat.py — SSE chat endpoint + session management
- NEW: app/agent_tools.py — bash/read/write/edit tool definitions for Claude API
- NEW: app/templates/_curator_panel.html — sidebar UI
- MODIFY: app/templates/reader.html — add Curator toggle + panel
- MODIFY: app/__init__.py — register blueprint
- MODIFY: requirements.txt — add anthropic SDK

## Dependencies
- Anthropic API key in server .env
- Claude API with tool use (streaming)
- Git installed on server (already is)

title: Auto-convert uploads to Karpathy LLM Wiki format
description:
## Context

When someone uploads a zip of markdown files (e.g., an Obsidian vault, a folder of dated notes, Jeremy Nixon's thinking index), can we auto-detect structure and post-process it into Karpathy's LLM Wiki format?

## Karpathy format target
- `raw/` — immutable source docs
- `wiki/` — LLM-generated/maintained pages with frontmatter (title, tags, status, confidence, contradictions, open_questions)
- `index.md` — content catalog with one-line summaries per page
- `log.md` — append-only activity log
- `schema.md` / `CLAUDE.md` — conventions and domain rules
- Cross-references: symmetric wikilinks, key claims, connections sections

## What we'd need to detect
- Date patterns in filenames or content (daily notes → log.md entries)
- Existing wikilinks `[[...]]` (Obsidian native)
- Folder structure heuristics (e.g., `daily/`, `templates/`, `attachments/`)
- Frontmatter already present vs needs generating
- Which files are "source material" vs "synthesis/wiki pages"

## Open questions
- **Option vs default?** Should this be opt-in ("Convert to LLM Wiki format") or automatic on upload?
- **Destructive or additive?** Do we reorganize their files into raw/wiki/, or just add the missing pieces (index.md, log.md, frontmatter) on top of existing structure?
- **LLM-powered or rule-based?** Simple heuristics might handle 80% (date parsing, wikilink extraction, folder detection). Full Karpathy format (key claims, contradictions, confidence scores) probably needs an LLM pass.
- **When does it run?** On zip upload? On git push? As an async "maintain" action?
- **How does this relate to WikiHub's built-in maintenance vision?** (see llm-wiki-ecosystem wiki — WikiHub Opportunity page)

## Possible approach
1. Rule-based first pass: detect dates, extract wikilinks, generate index.md, infer frontmatter from filenames
2. Optional LLM pass: classify pages as entity/concept/source, generate key claims, find contradictions
3. Surface as "Organize" button on wiki page, or auto-suggest after zip upload

title: Sidebar folders collapse when navigating via sidebar link — state not persisted on page load
description:
## Problem

When clicking a sidebar link to navigate to a new page, folders that were expanded on the previous page collapse on the new page. User loses their place in the tree each time they navigate.

## Root cause

`applyFolderState()` in `app/templates/reader.html` (around line 918) only respects folder open/closed state if the user explicitly clicked the toggle chevron (which writes to `localStorage['wikihub-sidebar-folders']`).

Folders that auto-expand because they contain the active page are opened server-side via the template's `{{ 'open' if item.active else '' }}` — this state is never written to localStorage. On the next page load (in a different folder), that folder's entry in localStorage is still missing, so it falls through to "non-active folders with no localStorage state stay collapsed".

Effect: every time you click through to a page in a different branch of the tree, the previously-active folder collapses.

## Suggested fix (NOT a major refactor — ~15 lines of JS)

In `applyFolderState()`, after deciding the state for each folder, write the final state back to localStorage. That way any folder that was open on page load (whether by explicit toggle or by auto-expand for active page) persists its openness through navigation.

Edge case to preserve: if the user explicitly collapsed a folder (`state[path] === false`), keep that intent. Only persist `true` for folders that end up open.

## Files

- `app/templates/reader.html` — `applyFolderState()` near line 918
- Same pattern exists in `app/templates/folder.html` and `app/templates/profile.html` — keep consistent
- Tag as `recurring` once fixed since sidebar JS is historically fragile (see wikihub-58c, wikihub-bnj, wikihub-9c8j)

## Related

- wikihub-a6l (closed): established auto-expand-to-active behavior
- wikihub-9c8j (open): sidebar reorders items on click (different bug, similar area)

## Verification

- Open a wiki, click a page deep in folder A. Confirm folder A expands.
- Click a page deep in folder B. Folder A should remain expanded. Currently it collapses.
close_reason: Persist folder open-state on page load so auto-expanded active folders stay open across navigation. Fixed in reader.html applyFolderState + folder.html/profile.html inline init blocks.

title: Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md with reserved subdomain list
description:
Per-user wiki subdomains: username.wikihub.md instead of wikihub.globalbr.ai/@user/slug.

## Product questions to resolve
1. What counts as a user's 'personal wiki'? Specific slug, default wiki, or first-created?
2. Do non-personal wikis get sub-subdomains (recipes.jacobcole.wikihub.md) or stay path-based?
3. How does @wikihub community index behave? (wikihub.wikihub.md is awkward)

## Required
- **Reserved subdomain list**: www, api, collab, staging, app, admin, dev, explore, help, docs, blog, about, status, community, wiki, wikihub (and more — architect will propose full list)
- **Wildcard DNS** (*.wikihub.md) — Cloudflare
- **Wildcard SSL cert** — Let's Encrypt DNS-01 via Cloudflare API, or Cloudflare Universal SSL
- **Nginx wildcard server block** on AWS Lightsail origin
- **Flask subdomain routing** — SERVER_NAME-based or Host header middleware
- **Username collision migration** — check existing usernames against reserved list
- **Backward compat** — /@user/slug URLs keep working (301 redirect to canonical subdomain)

title: Signup form not detected by password managers (1Password)
description:
## Problem

The signup / create account page is not recognized by 1Password (and likely other password managers) as an account creation form. 1Password does not offer to autofill or save credentials.

## Likely cause

Password managers detect signup forms via:
- `autocomplete="new-password"` on the password field
- `autocomplete="username"` or `autocomplete="email"` on the identity field
- `<form>` element wrapping the inputs
- Input `name` and `type` attributes (`type="email"`, `type="password"`, `name="email"`)

If any of these are missing or non-standard (e.g., using `<div>` instead of `<form>`, custom input components without proper attributes, or missing `autocomplete` hints), password managers won't detect it.

## Fix

Ensure the signup form has:
1. A `<form>` element wrapping all inputs
2. `<input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">` for the email field
3. `<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="new-password">` for the password field
4. `<input type="text" name="username" autocomplete="username">` for the username/display name field
5. A `<button type="submit">` (not just an onclick handler)

Test with 1Password browser extension after fixing — it should offer to save new credentials on submit.

title: Design history: ideaflow-vision wiki — scaffolding decisions
description:
Documentation of design decisions for the new `ideaflow-vision` wiki at https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision.

## Origin
Jacob asked to synthesize ~57 Slack messages tagged #explainingideaflow (2022→Feb 2026) plus future notestream captures into a coherent LLM wiki about the IdeaFlow vision. Separate from vision-convo (which is one conversation's snapshot).

## Claude's original proposal
- Name: `ideaflow-vision`
- Structure: index.md, AGENTS.md, raw/ by year-window, wiki/framings/ (one page per metaphor), wiki/pitches/ (audience-specific), wiki/themes/, wiki/sources/
- Synthesize Jacob's own framings, cross-link to vision-convo
- Skip notestream for v1

## Codex second opinion (gpt-5.4 high, session 019daa5f)
- ✅ Keep separate wiki (agreed)
- ⚠️ "Too axis-heavy" — don't make framing, audience, theme, era all first-class
- Recommended hybrid: primary=functional layers (personal/team/humanity), secondary=framings, single chronology.md, single entry-points.md
- 80–90% synthesis, 10–20% verbatim quotes
- Add page types: north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, chronology, canonical-terms, open-questions, use-cases
- Rename `pitches/` to `translations/` or `entry-points/` or `explainers/` — "pitches" reads salesy
- Risks: context collapse, internal language leakage, metaphor sprawl, product/worldview blur

## Jacob's decisions
1. **Keep `pitches/`** — overrule Codex's rename. Pitches is good.
2. **Use verbatim quotes heavily** — keep original lines, don't over-synthesize. Jacob's voice matters.
3. **Framing for evolution:** "The vision hasn't necessarily changed longitudinally, but it has been refined and has evolved and matured, and more forks have been discovered, with more details and realizations." → this is the backbone of chronology.md
4. Adopt Codex's missing page types (north-star-thesis, what-it-is-not, system-map, etc.)
5. Adopt Codex's functional-layer primary organization (personal → team → humanity)
6. Save this design history in the wiki itself (wiki/meta/design-history.md) and in this beads ticket

## Risk mitigations adopted
- Editorial note in AGENTS.md acknowledging synthesis of historical framings
- Date every ingested excerpt
- Separate canonical current framing from historical
- Link from framings/ pages back to raw/ with timestamps

## Related
- Vision-convo wiki: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/vision-convo
- Slack source archive (local-only): ~/code/ideaflow-slack-wiki/
- Original session: 1137ed19-6d0a-4294-9240-7c559603a947 (line 462)
- Codex session: 019daa5f-4a5c-7771-a654-a07d44510344

Reciprocal link added to vision-convo at projects/ideaflow.md.

title: Batch-import Jacob's Google Docs exports into /@jacobcole/jacobcole
description:
## ⚠️ REVIEW THIS PLAN BEFORE EXECUTING

Jacob exported ~253 markdown files from his Google Docs universe to
`/Users/jacobcole/code/DataExports/` (also in `~/Documents/vibe-coding-guide/DataExports/`
and `~/Documents/Personal/Book-Draft/source-material/google-docs-full/`).

Only 22 pages made it to prod (`/@jacobcole/jacobcole`). **Import never ran at scale.**

## Export inventory
- `markdown-by-hierarchy/` — 88 files, canonical tree structure (chronicpain/, foodslist/, ideaflow/, infrastructure/, manifestos/, misc/, philosophy/, thingsyoudidntknowexisted/, worldgestalts/, worldquestguild/)
- `markdown-by-category/` — 89 files, same docs re-grouped (duplicate of hierarchy)
- `markdown-new-missing-docs/` — 48 files, second-pass export catching what first pass missed
- `markdown-new-subdomains/` — 26 files, each satellite site (sleep/aspirations/lifehacks/healingartsgrant/etc.) as its own doc

After dedup: ~120 unique documents to import. Current: 22. Gap: ~100.

## Proposed plan (REVIEW)

**Target:** `/@jacobcole/jacobcole` (personal wiki)

**Steps:**
1. Write a batch importer script (`scripts/import_jacobcole_gdocs.py`) that walks a source tree and uploads to `POST /api/v1/wikis/jacobcole/jacobcole/pages`, preserving relative paths.
2. **Dry-run first.** Print every page it *would* create with size + path. No writes.
3. Sources in order (with dedup on normalized slug):
    - `markdown-by-hierarchy/` (primary, canonical tree)
    - `markdown-new-missing-docs/` (layer on, under `missing/` prefix OR same tree if paths match)
    - `markdown-new-subdomains/` (under `subdomains/` prefix — each satellite site as its own page)
    - **SKIP** `markdown-by-category/` (duplicate of hierarchy)
4. **Default visibility: `private`** — safer for a maximal personal brain dump. Jacob flips pages `public` selectively afterward.
5. **Conflict policy:** skip existing pages by default (don't clobber the 22 curated ones). Optional `--force` flag to overwrite.
6. Post-import: run a PII/privacy scanner on imported pages that lists any page containing:
    - Email addresses (`[\w\.]+@[\w\.-]+`)
    - Phone numbers (`\d{3}[- .]?\d{3}[- .]?\d{4}`)
    - Google Docs URLs (`docs\.google\.com/document/d/\S+`)
    - URLs to other private-ish satellite sites (`\*\.jacobcole\.net`, `\*\.jacobblog\.net`)
   So Jacob can review before flipping anything public.

## Flags to review explicitly before making public
These docs likely contain other people's info:
- `hiringblurb.md`, `hiringlist.md`, `hotconnections.md`, `housinglist.md`, `ilparty.md`
- `shadirecs.md` (shadi recommendations)
- `people/*` (if any)
- `productfeedback/bugslist.md`, `productfeedback/productfeedback.md` (internal ideaflow)

## Out of scope for this ticket
- Recursively fetching the satellite subdomain docs fresh from the web (already in `markdown-new-subdomains/` from the export)
- Breaking into multiple wikis (e.g. `/@jacobcole/health-wellness`). Can be done later by moving pages.
- Visibility flips — Jacob reviews the PII report and flips manually.

## Backup before execution
Prod backup from 2026-04-20 already exists at `~/wikihub-backups/prod-2026-04-20-020552/` (db dump + app tar + nginx + SSL). Good safety net if import misbehaves.

title: Rename visibility levels for clarity: public→public-view, unlisted→unlisted-view
description:
## Problem

The current visibility names are confusing. 'public' doesn't make it clear that it means read-only (no editing). Users could reasonably think 'public' means anyone can edit.

## Proposed Naming

| Current | New | Meaning |
|---------|-----|---------|
| public | public-view | Anyone can read, only owner can edit |
| public-edit | public-edit | Anyone can read AND edit |
| unlisted | unlisted-view | Only people with the link can read |
| private | private | Only owner/ACL can read |

## Scope

- Update the `VALID_VISIBILITIES` set in `app/acl.py`
- Update all references in routes, templates, models
- Update frontmatter parsing to accept both old and new names (backwards compat)
- Update the visibility badge labels in reader.html, folder.html
- Update API docs
- Migration: existing pages with 'public' should map to 'public-view', 'unlisted' to 'unlisted-view'
- Keep accepting old names in frontmatter for backwards compatibility (Postel's Law)

## Files involved
- `app/acl.py` — VALID_VISIBILITIES
- `app/routes/api_wikis.py` — visibility endpoints
- `app/templates/reader.html` — visibility badges
- `app/templates/folder.html` — visibility badges
- `app/models.py` — any visibility defaults
- `wikihub-spec-final-2026-04-08.md` — update spec

## Notes
Candidate for Codex — thorough rename across codebase.

title: Hover preview cards leak when user moves to another link before fetch completes
description: In app/templates/base.html hover preview JS, openPreview() pushes a loading card onto stack then fetches. If user hovers a new link before fetch resolves, closeFromDepth() pops the loading card, but the fetch callback still appends a real card to document.body without re-adding it to stack. The orphan card has no owner and cannot be closed. Fix: in the fetch .then callback, after findIndex, return early if idx < 0 (stale response).

title: Sidebar: level-2 children not indented past parent (wikihub-58c regression)
description:
## Problem

Children of folders at the 2nd nesting level appear at the same x-position as their parent folder, not visibly indented underneath it.

User report (2026-04-20): "The children of a folder need to be indented farther underneath it. Right now they're at the same level ... at least true on a secondary level, second level down."

## Root cause

In app/templates/reader.html:
- `.sidebar-folder-toggle { width: 20px }`  (line 45)
- `.sidebar-children { padding-left: 20px }` (line 52)

These are EQUAL. A folder-row is `[toggle 20px][item]`, so the parent's folder-icon sits at x=20+8 = 28px inside the folder. A child file row inside .sidebar-children has no toggle, so its item starts at x=20px inside the parent folder — which means the file's icon lands at exactly the same 28px as the parent's icon. No visible nesting.

## Why the regression test didn't catch it

tests/test_e2e.py:636 (`test_sidebar_indentation`) checks only:
1. `.sidebar-children` CSS exists with `padding-left >= 16px` — passes (it's 20)
2. `<div class="sidebar-children">` appears in HTML — passes

It does not verify that `padding-left > toggle-width`, which is the real invariant.

## Fix

1. Bump `.sidebar-children` padding-left to 28px (or more) so child items are clearly indented past the parent's icon.
2. Strengthen the regression test to enforce `sidebar-children.padding-left > sidebar-folder-toggle.width + minimum visual gap`.

## Related

- Parent ticket: wikihub-58c (closed, tagged recurring)
- Attaching recurring label.
close_reason: Fixed: bumped .sidebar-children padding-left from 20px→28px in reader.html (20px==toggle width, so children had zero visible indent at level 2+). Strengthened test_sidebar_indentation to assert padding > toggle_width + 4. Verified with chrome-devtools: depth-1 folder icons at x=66, depth-2 file icons at x=74 (8px visible shift). 23/23 tests pass.

title: Subdomain assignment strategy — auto-claim vs opt-in, collision policy, opt-out
description:
Current (shipped in wikihub-3fjb): subdomains are OPT-IN per wiki. Owner claims at /@user/slug/settings. jacobcole has 15 wikis, only rsi-wiki claimed a subdomain.

## Options on the table

### 1. Keep opt-in (current)
- Wikis stay at <user>.wikihub.md/<slug> until owner claims a subdomain.
- Pros: no automatic collisions, no migration needed, user controls branding.
- Cons: most wikis never get the nicer URL; wiki subdomain feature is underused.

### 2. Auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served
- On POST /api/v1/wikis, try to claim the slug as a subdomain. If collision, fall back to empty (still accessible under user subdomain path).
- Pros: most wikis auto-get the nice URL. Aligns with jacob's maximalist preference.
- Cons: early users hog common slugs (ideas, notes, recipes, etc.). Collision = silent fallback unless we surface it.

### 3. Auto-claim with <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (sub-subdomains)
- Every wiki auto-gets <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md (no global collisions).
- Pros: no collisions ever, every wiki gets a nice branded URL.
- Cons: nginx + Cloudflare wildcard-of-wildcard complexity. Cloudflare doesn't support *.*.domain — requires SaaS addon or per-user DNS records. Long URLs.

### 4. Hybrid: auto-claim with namespace fallback
- Try <slug>.wikihub.md first. If taken, auto-assign <slug>.<user>.wikihub.md.
- Pros: common slugs still work for the first claimer, everyone else gets namespaced.
- Cons: mixed URL schemes across the product. Inconsistency.

### 5. Bring-your-own-domain (BYOD)
- Let users CNAME their own domain (e.g., notes.example.com → wikihub origin).
- Pros: max flexibility, no wikihub.md URL at all.
- Cons: per-domain SSL provisioning (Caddy on-demand, or Cloudflare for SaaS), DNS ops.

## User control primitives needed regardless
- [ ] Opt-out of subdomain (nullable already — setting to null works)
- [ ] Rename / change subdomain post-creation (API already supports — works)
- [ ] Reserved-word enforcement (already in subdomains.py RESERVED_SUBDOMAINS)
- [ ] Username-collision check (already checks via username_conflicts)

## Personal recommendation (for review)
Start with #2 (auto-claim at creation, first-come-first-served) for MVP. Surface collisions in the settings UI: "subdomain <X> is taken, claim something else or keep path URL". Collect feedback, layer in #3 or #5 only if needed.

## Decision owner
jacobcole — opinion TBD, initial lean is #2.

## Related
- Closed: wikihub-3fjb (initial opt-in implementation shipped 2026-04-20)
- Open: real-time collab (wikihub-w0s4) which targets staging.wikihub.md

title: Landing page: 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' CTA
description:
Consider adding secondary CTAs on the landing page hero alongside 'Publish a wiki in seconds'. Candidates:

1. **'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity'** — the grand vision, taps into Snow Crash / Librarian philosophy. wikihub isn't just a tool, it's a collective knowledge project.

2. **'Make private collaborative lists with agents'** — the practical agent use case. Not everything is public publishing — private wikis where your agent writes and organizes alongside you.

3. **'Agent-native Google Drive'** — positions wikihub as the file system / knowledge layer that agents can actually read and write to, unlike Google Drive. Markdown files in git = structured, versionable, API-accessible. Google Drive is for humans with browsers; wikihub is for humans AND agents. This framing resonates with the 'why not just use Notion/Google Docs' objection — those tools have terrible agent APIs.

These could be secondary CTAs, tagline variants, rotating hero text, or value prop cards below the fold. The key insight: wikihub has three distinct value props for three audiences:
- **Publishers** → 'Publish a wiki in seconds' (Karpathy wave, Obsidian users)
- **Visionaries** → 'Contribute to the Archive of Humanity' (knowledge builders, researchers)
- **Agent users** → 'Agent-native Google Drive' or 'Collaborate with agents on private knowledge' (developers, AI power users)

title: WebSocket support for xterm.js terminal and Curator chat
description:
## Problem

The admin terminal (xterm.js) uses HTTP polling (100ms GET + POST per keystroke), causing ~200ms lag per character. The Curator chat uses SSE which ties up a gunicorn sync worker.

## Solution

Add WebSocket support via flask-sock. This gives:
1. Real-time terminal I/O (admin auth page)
2. Real-time Curator chat streaming (instead of SSE)
3. Foundation for future real-time features

## Requirements
- Add flask-sock to requirements.txt
- Switch gunicorn to gevent workers: `gunicorn -k gevent`
- WebSocket endpoint for terminal: /ws/terminal
- WebSocket endpoint for Curator chat: /ws/curator
- Update xterm.js to use WebSocket attach addon
- Update Curator panel JS to use WebSocket instead of fetch+SSE

## Risk
Changing gunicorn worker type from sync to gevent could affect request handling. Test all existing endpoints after the switch.

title: Hover preview: consider bumping max stacking depth by +1 (3 → 4)
description:
Follow-up to wikihub-5613 stack-leak fixes. Current behavior: unlimited recursion on related-link hovers (no depth cap). Proposed baseline: cap at depth 3, past which the related link behaves as a normal hyperlink (no preview spawns, click still navigates). This ticket tracks the design question of whether to bump that cap to 4.

Arguments for +1:
- users who want to explore a concept neighborhood 'one more step' get it
- 4 cards is still manageable in terms of screen real estate for most laptops (3×~320px wide = fits comfortably; 4 starts to push on right edge but alternating below/above placement helps)
- with current fixes (timer leaks fixed, mouseleave suppression narrowed, orphan sweep, click-outside always closes), the reliability cost of one more level is low

Arguments against +1 (stay at 3):
- 3 is LessWrong's practical behavior; matches the inspiration
- beyond 3, the user is probably better served by clicking through to a full page
- more cards = more overlap edge cases to worry about
- the fourth card's spatial positioning becomes awkward (limited below/above slots left)

Decision gate: try cap=3 in production first, collect feedback (or just run for a week), then decide whether to bump. One-line implementation regardless: in the related-link mouseenter, early-return when depth+1 >= CAP.

See also: wikihub-5613 (the stack-leak fixes) — that work fixed reliability but did not introduce any cap. Whatever cap ships, write a small e2e test that POSTs a page with nested wikilinks and verifies the nth hover-preview-link's mouseenter does not spawn an (n+1)th card.

title: Curator Agent: inline sidebar agent for wiki curation (Snow Crash / Ready Player One inspired)
description:
## Vision

An inline curator agent sidebar in the spirit of the Librarian from Snow Crash and the Curator from Ready Player One. An AI assistant that lives in the WikiHub UI sidebar and can take actions on your behalf — curate, cross-reference, promote content between wikis.

## Example Use Case

You're browsing someone's idea list. You want to promote an idea to the Featured Wikis page. The curator agent can:
1. Read the current page context (who you are, what page you're on)
2. Take actions with your permissions (read/write wikis you have access to)
3. Append content to public-edit pages, create pages in your own wikis, etc.

## Architecture Considerations

### Agent Backend
- **Claude Code under the hood** (like Noos project) — coding agent with file/API tools
- Should run in **print mode** for streaming responses to the sidebar
- Needs the current user's API key for WikiHub API calls
- Must be aware of: logged-in user, current page URL, current wiki context

### Security / Isolation
- Agent runs with the permissions of the current user (their API key)
- Must NOT get access to wrong directories on the server
- **MVP: Unix user permissions** may be sufficient — each agent session runs as a restricted user with only API access (no filesystem access beyond temp dirs)
- **Future: Docker containers** for full isolation if needed
- Agent should only interact via the WikiHub API, not touch git repos directly

### Parallel Agents
- Architecture should support multiple users running curator agents simultaneously
- Each agent session is stateless — gets user context at start, runs, returns result
- Server-side: queue or spawn model per request

### UI
- Sidebar panel on the right side of wiki pages
- Chat-like interface: user types a request, agent streams a response
- Agent can show proposed changes before applying them
- Minimizable/collapsible

### Chrome Extension (v2)
- Personal agent sidebar that works on any website
- If Chrome extension sidebar is open, WikiHub's built-in sidebar hides (avoid duplication)
- Extension would use the same WikiHub API key
- Relates to the Cortex project for personal agent infrastructure
- Could detect if user is logged into WikiHub and auto-configure

## MVP Scope (v1)
1. Right sidebar panel on wiki pages (behind feature flag or logged-in only)
2. Chat input → sends to Claude API with system prompt including page context
3. Agent can call WikiHub API endpoints (search, read_page, create_page, update_page, append_section)
4. Streams response back to sidebar
5. No filesystem access — API-only

## Open Questions
- Unix permissions vs Docker for agent isolation?
- Should the agent have its own API key or use the user's?
- Rate limiting per user?
- How to handle the Chrome extension ↔ built-in sidebar coordination?

title: Epic: Obsidian feature parity audit — autocomplete, page creation, conventions
Cross-wiki wikilinks: [[@owner/slug/path]] syntax with hover previews
description:
Support `[[wikilinks]]` across wikis, not just within one wiki. Today `[[Page Title]]` resolves within the current wiki; cross-wiki links require full URLs (`https://wikihub.md/@owner/slug/path`) which skip the rich hover-preview treatment.

## Why it matters

1. **Hover previews across wikis.** WikiHub's hover-preview cards only fire for internal wikilinks. Users crossing between wikis (e.g., ideaflow-vision → vision-convo) lose the preview UX and get raw anchor tags instead.
2. **Rename resilience.** Full URLs are brittle: rename a page and every outbound link from a sister wiki breaks silently. Cross-wiki wikilinks (resolved against canonical IDs or current titles) survive renames.
3. **Unified backlink graph.** The `wikilinks` table / page-graph today stops at wiki boundaries. Cross-wiki wikilinks would make a user's multi-wiki network legible as one graph — which is exactly the worldview WikiHub is hosting.
4. **Authoring ergonomics.** Writing `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow]]` beats copy-pasting long URLs, especially in LLM-authored content.

## Triggering example

Just scaffolded `/@jacobcole/ideaflow-vision` with 15+ cross-links to `/@jacobcole/vision-convo`. All use full `https://wikihub.md/...` URLs. None trigger hover previews. If vision-convo ever renames `projects/ideaflow.md`, every outbound link from ideaflow-vision breaks.

## Proposed syntax (pick one — need decision)

1. **Path-qualified:** `[[@owner/slug/path/to/page]]` — explicit, unambiguous, closest to the URL shape. Display text defaults to page title; pipe for alias: `[[@jacobcole/vision-convo/projects/ideaflow|IdeaFlow]]`.
2. **Prefix-qualified:** `[[wiki:owner/slug:Page Title]]` — MediaWiki-style interwiki prefix. Less URL-like, more title-driven.
3. **Scheme-prefixed:** `[[wh://owner/slug/path]]` — most future-proof if we ever want other schemes (e.g., `gh://` for GitHub).

Recommend option 1 — closest to what users already see in URLs.

## Semantics to decide

- **Title vs path resolution.** Do we match the target wiki's page by path (stable, URL-like) or by title (friendlier, changes with renames)?
- **Permission handling.** If the target page is private, the link should render but hover preview should 404 gracefully (not leak metadata).
- **Rendering fallback.** If the target wiki doesn't exist or the page isn't found, render as a broken-wikilink (`color-wikilink-broken`) rather than a raw URL.
- **Backlink visibility.** Should `/@vision-convo/projects/ideaflow` see its inbound cross-wiki links from `/@ideaflow-vision/*` in its backlinks panel?

## Implementation sketch

Touch points:
- `app/renderer.py` — markdown-it-py wikilink plugin needs to recognize the cross-wiki pattern, build the correct URL, and mark the link for hover preview.
- `app/models.py` — `wikilinks` table currently scoped to one wiki; needs nullable `target_wiki_id` to represent cross-wiki edges.
- Hover preview fetcher in `app/templates/base.html` — currently hits `/api/v1/wikis/<current>/pages/...`; needs to honor a data attribute or URL pattern to switch wiki.
- Backlinks query — join across wikis when target page has cross-wiki backrefs.

## Out of scope (v1)

- Cross-account renames (what if target user renames their account).
- Private-to-private resolution (requires share-grant check at render time).
- Drag-and-drop authoring UI.

## Related

- Scaffolded the two-wiki setup that surfaced this: `ideaflow-vision` ↔ `vision-convo` on 2026-04-20. See `wikihub-4i3b` (closed).
- Vision-convo's `themes/llm-wiki-as-medium.md` already notes WikiHub as the IDE — cross-wiki linking is part of that IDE story.

## Priority justification

P2 — not blocking current work (full URLs work), but every day we don't have this, the cross-wiki graph gets staler and links get more fragile. Becomes P1 when a third wiki joins the network or a cross-wiki rename breaks something visibly.

title: Sidebar: collapsible folders that auto-expand to current page
description:
## Problem

When navigating directly to a deep page like /@wikihub/wikihub/philosophy/The Librarian, the sidebar shows the folder tree but:
1. Folders aren't collapsible — the full tree is always expanded or always collapsed
2. When landing on a nested page, the relevant folder isn't auto-opened to show where you are

## Expected behavior

- Sidebar folders should be collapsible (click to toggle open/closed)
- When navigating directly to a page, the folder containing that page should auto-expand
- Other folders should default to collapsed, keeping the sidebar clean
- The current page should be visually highlighted and scrolled into view

## Files involved

- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro + JS for toggle
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same sidebar rendering
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` already sets `active` flag on items

## Notes

The toggle JS (`toggleFolder()`) and CSS (`.sidebar-folder.open`) already exist. The issue is that the default open/closed state isn't intelligently set based on the current page path.
close_reason: Sidebar folders collapse by default, auto-expand to active page, scroll active item into view. Consistent JS across reader.html, folder.html, profile.html.

title: Agents over-create wikis; should favor pages/folders in existing wikis
description:
## Observation

Agents (concrete example: Open Asks work on 2026-04-18, session creating /@jacobcole/asks) default to creating a new wiki when asked for a "section" about a topic. A wiki in WikiHub is a full git repository — bare repo + public mirror + ACL + audit log + sidebar entry on the user profile. That is heavy. A page of open asks does not justify a separate repo.

## Correct default

Create a page (or folder of pages) within an existing wiki. Reach for a new wiki only when:

- The content is a distinct, standalone body of work with its own identity and lifecycle
- It will plausibly be forked, starred, or cloned as a unit
- It has a distinct collaborator set (different ACL)
- It is a wiki *about* a topic, not a page *on* a topic

For Jacob's use case, "Open Asks" is a page; "admitsphere" or "rsi-wiki" are wikis.

## Contrast with Noos

Noos list nodes are the opposite: cheap, encouraged to proliferate, one per organizational concept. The graph absorbs the sprawl because edges do the work. Agents should pattern-match to the grain of each system — page-first in WikiHub, list-freely in Noos. See the paired Noos issue for the philosophical half of this.

## Action items

- [ ] Update AGENTS.md: add a "when to create a new wiki vs. a page" section with the heuristic above
- [ ] Consider surfacing folder-based organization more prominently in the UI (page tree sidebar exists; the create-page UX could nudge toward `folder/page.md` paths)
- [ ] Migrate /@jacobcole/asks content to a single page inside an existing wiki (probably thoughtstream/asks.md); delete the standalone asks wiki once content is moved and redirects (if any) are in place
- [ ] Decide whether llms.txt / AGENTS.md route guidance should include this heuristic so connected agents pick it up

## Related

- Noos twin of this ticket (philosophical reference): to be created
- Concrete artifact of the mistake: https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@jacobcole/asks (scheduled for migration)

title: Explore Editorial Picks keep getting clobbered — need data-driven curation
description:
## Problem (recurring)

The Explore page's Editorial Picks section keeps losing its curated wikis. This has happened multiple times. The picks are currently hardcoded in main.py as a list of (owner, slug) tuples, which means any refactor to the explore route can accidentally drop them.

## Current Fix

Hardcoded _EDITORIAL_PICKS list in app/routes/main.py with curated wikis:
- wikihub/wikihub, jacobcole/ideas, jacobcole/quotes, jacobcole/rsi-wiki, jacobcole/admitsphere, jacobcole/llm-wiki-ecosystem, harrisonqian/tools-stack

## Better Long-term Solution

Move editorial picks to a data source (not code):
1. A DB table `editorial_picks` with wiki_id + sort_order
2. Or a config file / admin endpoint to manage picks
3. Or derive from the Featured Wikis page in wikihub/wikihub wiki

This way code refactors can't accidentally drop curated content.

## How to Verify

Visit /explore and check that the "Editorial Picks" section shows multiple wiki cards, not just WikiHub.

## Problem

The left sidebar is not accessible on mobile. There's no hamburger menu or toggle button to open it. This means mobile users can't navigate between pages within a wiki — they're stuck on whatever page they landed on.

## Scope

1. **Sidebar toggle on mobile** — add a hamburger/menu button that opens the sidebar as an overlay on small screens
2. **Basic mobile UX audit** — verify all core flows work on mobile:
   - Reading pages (text wrapping, tables, code blocks)
   - Navigation (breadcrumbs, sidebar, wikilinks)
   - Profile page layout
   - Search
   - Login/signup forms
   - Creating/editing pages (if logged in)

## Files involved
- `app/templates/reader.html` — has sidebar-overlay infrastructure but may lack a toggle button
- `app/templates/folder.html` — same
- `app/templates/base.html` — global nav bar, may need hamburger
- CSS in each template's style block — media queries for mobile

title: Design community index wiki (@wikihub as curated meta-index)
description:
The @wikihub/wikihub wiki should serve as a curated community index — an 'awesome list of awesome lists.' User stories:

1. A user creates a great 'Best Body Masters List' wiki. They want it featured.
2. The community wiki has a page like 'Best Fitness Wikis' that links to it with a blurb.
3. A visitor lands on @wikihub and can browse curated categories pointing to the best user wikis.

This is different from /explore (algorithmic, by stars/activity). The community wiki is editorial — someone decides what's featured.

Key questions to resolve:
- Is @wikihub/wikihub the right place for this, or should there be a separate @wikihub/featured wiki?
- How do categories work? Pages in the wiki like 'Best AI Tools Wikis', 'Best Reading Lists'?
- Should featured wikis get a badge/indicator on their own pages?
- Who can edit the community index? Just admins, or suggested-edit PRs from users?
- How does this interplay with the Explore page? Does Explore pull from the community wiki's editorial picks?

For v1: probably just manually curated pages in @wikihub/wikihub that link to user wikis with [[cross-wiki wikilinks]] or plain URLs. The index IS the wiki — no special machinery needed. Editorial curation is just writing markdown.

Reference: ListHub had similar brainstorming about community lists as indexes of other lists.

title: Inline expansion of own wikis in left sidebar (no navigation needed)
description:
## Goal
In the left-hand sidebar on a user's profile page (e.g. /@jacobcole), allow expanding the tree of a wiki inline to see its pages, without having to first navigate into the wiki.

## Current behavior
Sidebar shows top-level wikis grouped by folder (health/, ideas/, local/, etc.) and a flat list of featured wikis. To see pages inside a wiki you have to click into it.

## Proposed behavior
Click a disclosure chevron next to a wiki name to expand it in place, showing the same page tree that would appear in the wiki's own sidebar (respecting ACLs / visibility). Collapsing returns to the compact view. State persists in localStorage so the tree stays as you left it across visits.

## Open questions / design considerations
- Scale: some wikis have hundreds of pages. Lazy-load page tree on first expand; cap initial depth; show "view all" affordance.
- Confusion risk: mixing wiki-level nav and page-level nav in one tree could muddle mental model. Maybe render expanded children indented with a subtle background so they read as "drilled in" rather than siblings of wikis.
- ACL: only show pages the current viewer can see (same rules as the wiki's own sidebar).
- Mobile: sidebar is already cramped; may need to stay collapsed-by-default on narrow viewports.
- Performance: fetch via the same endpoint used by the wiki's own sidebar; cache per-session.

## V2 scope
This is likely a V2 polish, not a V1 requirement — useful for owners navigating their own knowledge faster, and for visitors skimming a profile. Worth prototyping behind a feature flag to see if it's clarifying or overwhelming.

## Motivation
User (Jacob) has 10+ wikis and finds navigating into each one to see contents slow. Inline expansion would turn the profile into a live directory of his whole public knowledge base.
close_reason: Implemented. Profile sidebar Wikis section now has a chevron on each wiki; click fetches /@user/slug/sidebar.json and renders the tree inline with subtle left-border nesting. Folder paths namespaced as slug|path to avoid localStorage collisions across trees. Expanded state persists across reloads. Verified with chrome-devtools: expand, nested folder open, persistence restore all work. 19 e2e tests pass.

title: Sidebar: distinguish folders from cross-wiki links in navigation
description:
## Problem

When navigating the sidebar, there's no visual distinction between:
1. **Folders** — subfolders within the current wiki (e.g. `ideas/` folder inside `jacobcole/jacobcole`)
2. **Wiki links** — links that take you to a different wiki entirely (e.g. from personal wiki to `jacobcole/ideas`)

Both use the same folder/page icons and indentation. Users can't tell if clicking will keep them in the current wiki or jump to a different one.

Currently the sidebar only shows items from the current wiki's file tree. Cross-wiki links only appear in page content as regular markdown links. The profile page has a separate "Wikis" section, but this pattern isn't used elsewhere.

## Proposed Solutions (pick one or combine)

### Option A: Wiki icon in sidebar (minimal)
Add a third icon type — a small "wiki" icon (e.g. book/globe/grid) — distinct from the folder and page icons. When the sidebar lists sub-wikis (e.g. on a profile or index page), use this icon instead of folder.

### Option B: "Your wikis" sidebar section
Like the profile page already does — show a separate labeled section "WIKIS" below the file tree in the sidebar. This already works on profile.html (lines 176-185). Extend to reader.html and folder.html when viewing a user's personal wiki.

### Option C: Breadcrumb-aware context switching
When you click from one wiki into another, the breadcrumb should make it visually obvious you've changed context — e.g. the sidebar header changes from "JACOB'S IDEAS" to the new wiki's name, with a "← back to profile" link. (This already happens, but the transition is jarring with no warning.)

### Option D: Wiki cards in page content
When the index page links to other wikis, render those as visually distinct cards (title + description + page count) rather than plain text links. This makes it obvious in the content area that these are whole wikis, not pages.

## Recommendation
Start with **Option B** — it's the simplest and profile.html already has the pattern. Then add **Option A** (wiki icon) for polish. Option D is nice for landing pages but is a bigger change.

## Files involved
- `app/routes/wiki.py` — `_build_sidebar_tree()` builds the sidebar
- `app/templates/reader.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 181-209)
- `app/templates/folder.html` — sidebar rendering macro (lines 47-69)
- `app/templates/profile.html` — already has the "Wikis" section pattern (lines 176-185)
close_reason: Implemented Option B + A: sidebar now shows a 'Jacob Cole's Wikis' section with book icons below the current wiki's file tree. Applied to reader.html, folder.html, and profile.html. Deployed to production.

title: TOC double-encodes HTML entities (ampersand shows as &)
description:
The table of contents in the right sidebar double-encodes HTML entities. Headings like 'Homerow & Vimium' render correctly in the main content but show as 'Homerow & Vimium' in the TOC.

Root cause: extract_toc() in renderer.py (line 38) strips HTML tags but doesn't unescape HTML entities. The resulting text (containing &) is then escaped again by Jinja's auto-escaping, producing &amp; in the HTML.

Fix: add html.unescape(text) after the re.sub() call in extract_toc().

Reproduction: visit https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/@harrisonqian/tools-stack and look at CONTENTS sidebar — 'Homerow & Vimium' and 'Notes & Knowledge'
close_reason: Duplicate of wikihub-osd (same TOC double-encoding bug)

title: Restructure Jeremy Nixon wiki into Karpathy three-layer format
description:
Jeremy's 452 pages are currently flat in the wiki root. The Karpathy pattern organizes into:

- schema.md — what this wiki is about, how it's structured
- index.md — table of contents (already exists)
- log.md — timeline of additions
- wiki/ — compiled, curated knowledge pages by topic
- raw/ — unprocessed source entries (the current 450 dated posts)

An agent could:
1. Move all dated entries into raw/
2. Create wiki/ pages that compile the best insights by topic (machine intelligence, learning, startups, thinking frameworks, etc.)
3. Generate schema.md describing the wiki structure
4. Create log.md with a timeline

This is a good showcase of the 'agent maintains a wiki' use case — the raw material exists, an LLM compiles it into structured knowledge.

title: Define onboarding artifact map for world vision, life paradigm, and IdeaFlow
description:
Create the next-step content plan for turning the Vision Convo wiki into clear onboarding materials.

Context:
The raw conversation and wiki already contain the major ideas, but the material is still distributed across themes, concepts, projects, practices, and resources. The immediate need is not more extraction; it is a clear onboarding architecture.

Goal:
Define the minimum set of public-facing artifacts and their relationship to each other so a new reader can understand:
1. the broad world vision
2. the personal life paradigm underneath it
3. where IdeaFlow fits as the concrete implementation wedge

Recommended output of this task:
- A short artifact map covering at least these documents:
  - Onboarding to the Vision for the World
  - Onboarding to My Life Paradigm
  - What IdeaFlow Is Really For
  - Skeptic's FAQ
  - Reading / Watching / Doing Path
- For each artifact: audience, purpose, tone, and 5-10 bullet outline
- A recommended reading order / onboarding ladder for new people
- A note on what belongs in each layer:
  - worldview / civilization thesis
  - personal practice / life paradigm
  - product / company wedge
- Guidance on what should be explicit but separated:
  - core thesis vs speculative material
  - inspiration vs practical next steps
  - system map vs personal story

Why this matters:
Without this artifact map, the worldview, practices, and product wedge can blur together. That makes onboarding harder and makes IdeaFlow easier to misread as either just a startup with vague philosophy or a total worldview disguised as a startup.

Suggested framing:
- Life paradigm = what kind of human to become
- World vision = what kind of civilization to build
- IdeaFlow = the concrete first lever / implementation wedge

This is a planning/content-architecture task first. Implementation can happen as follow-on writing tickets once the artifact map is approved.
acceptance_criteria: A documented artifact map exists that names the core onboarding documents, explains the role of each, separates worldview vs life paradigm vs IdeaFlow, and proposes a reading order for new readers. The plan should be specific enough that each artifact can then be spun out into its own writing ticket.

title: Per-person sharing: wiki settings page with grant management
Graph system: page graph, full graph, filtered graph with progressive coloring
description:
## Context

Wikis are graphs. The wikilink structure already contains rich relationship data — we just need to visualize it. This spec defines a three-view graph system that works on any wiki (structure-agnostic) but looks better with metadata (progressive enhancement).

## Three views, one graph engine

Underlying data: nodes = pages, edges = wikilinks. Derived from the wikilinks index wikihub already builds for backlinks. No separate graph database needed.

### 1. Page graph (every page sidebar/footer)
- Current page = center node, highlighted
- Show all pages directly linked TO or FROM this page (1-hop)
- Optionally expand to 2-hop (friends of friends)
- Click node → navigate to that page
- Like Obsidian's local graph — scoped, useful, not overwhelming
- Must be fast enough to render inline on every page load

### 2. Full graph (wiki homepage + dedicated route)
- Every page is a node, every wikilink is an edge
- Force-directed layout — clusters emerge naturally from link density
- The "wow" view showing the shape of the whole knowledge base
- Zoom, pan, search, click-to-navigate
- Shown on wiki homepage (index.md) as an interactive embed and at a dedicated `/@user/wiki/graph` route

### 3. Filtered graph (overlays on full graph)
- Filter by: directory path, frontmatter `type:`, tag, or search query
- "Show only pages connected to X" — page graph launched from full graph
- "Hide all conversation pages" — reduce noise to see topic structure
- Toggle filters on/off, combine them

## Coloring strategy (progressive enhancement)

The graph looks good with zero schema knowledge and great with it:

| Signal | How to use | Agnostic? |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Directory path | `wiki/courses/` = blue, `wiki/topics/` = green, etc. | Yes — any wiki with folders |
| Frontmatter `type:` | Override directory color if present, more precise | Semi — only typed wikis |
| Frontmatter `tags:` | Secondary color ring or icon badge | Semi |
| Link count | Node size scales with inbound + outbound links | Yes — derived from wikilinks |
| Recency | Opacity or glow based on `updated:` or git date | Semi |

**Fallback:** no directories, no frontmatter, no nothing → every node same color, sized by link count. Still works. Degrades gracefully.

**Key rule:** never require metadata for the graph to function. Only use it to enhance when present.

## Where graphs appear

- **Page sidebar/footer**: page graph (1-hop), always visible, lightweight
- **Wiki homepage (index.md)**: full graph embedded inline — interactive `<canvas>` or collapsible section. WikiHub auto-injects on index.md pages, or expose a `{{graph}}` embed directive
- **Dedicated `/graph` route**: full-screen interactive graph with filter controls, per wiki

The homepage embed doesn't violate structure-agnosticism — it's a rendering feature of the homepage route, not imposed on content.

## Tech notes

- **Data source:** wikilinks index (already exists for backlinks). Endpoints: `GET /api/v1/wikis/:owner/:slug/graph` returning `{nodes: [{id, title, type?, dir?}], edges: [{source, target}]}`
- **Frontend:** d3-force or similar force-directed layout. Lightweight, browser-native.
- **Performance:** wikis under 500 pages — render all nodes. Above that — progressive loading or cluster-first-then-expand. Page graph (1-hop) must be instant.
- **Interaction:** hover = show title + link count, click = navigate, drag = reposition, scroll = zoom

title: Session cookie missing Secure and SameSite flags
description: Session cookie is set with only HttpOnly; Path=/. Missing: Secure flag (required for HTTPS to prevent transmission over HTTP), SameSite=Lax or Strict (CSRF prevention). Fix: set SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True and SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax' in Flask config.
close_reason: added SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE (env-configurable, defaults on), SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE=Lax, SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True

title: Noos design question (reference): heavier base concept vs. list-of-lists
description:
## Cross-project reference

This ticket exists on the WikiHub side for visibility; the actual design question lives on Noos.

## The question

Does Noos need a heavier unit above `list` — a "base" or "space" with distinct identity, visibility boundary, export surface, or collaborator set? Today Noos is uniform: nodes + edges, with `list` as the container type and hierarchy built from list-of-lists.

## Why WikiHub cares

WikiHub's "wiki" is exactly that heavier unit — a git repo with its own ACL, mirror, and identity. The contrast between the two systems is informative either way: if Noos adds a heavier unit, agent guidance needs to mirror WikiHub's "page-first" heuristic. If Noos stays flat, the guidance stays "list-freely."

## Link

Noos side of the discussion: will be pinned here once created.

Status: reference only. Track the decision on the Noos side; echo outcomes back to this ticket.

title: Connection system: cross-referencing patterns that prevent noise
description:
## Context

Cross-references are the whole point of a wiki over flat notes. But random/spurious connections make it worse than no connections. This issue defines a connection system that maximizes signal and minimizes noise.

Tested on a 115-page wiki — the patterns below emerged from what worked and what didn't.

## The problem

Agents generating wiki pages tend to either:
- Add zero connections (each page is an island)
- Add random connections based on keyword overlap ("both mention 'learning'" → spurious link)

Both are bad. We need inline wikilinks woven naturally into prose — like Wikipedia, not like a "see also" appendix.

## How connections work: inline wikilinks in prose

Links are woven into the text naturally as the page is written:

> "Harrison worked at [[orbit-internship|Orbit]] where [[mark-khrapko|Mark Khrapko]] mentored him on [[hiring-and-teams|hiring philosophy]] and he developed the [[cnn-spectrogram-analysis|CNN spectrogram classifier]]"

No dedicated "connections" section. If a relationship matters, it belongs in the prose. If it's not worth mentioning in prose, it's not a real connection. Backlinks (auto-generated by renderer) handle the reverse direction.

### Rules for inline linking

- **Link on first mention only** — don't link the same page multiple times in one section
- **Use display text** — `[[slug|Natural Text]]` so prose reads naturally
- **Don't over-link** — link when it adds navigational value, not every noun
- **Don't self-link** — a page never links to itself
- **Conservative matching** — better to miss a link than create a wrong one. "Mark" alone is ambiguous; "Mark Khrapko" is not.

Agents creating wiki pages should include inline links AS THEY WRITE, not as a separate pass. The schema/CLAUDE.md should specify this behavior.

## Cross-section connection taxonomy

Agents should specifically look for these high-value connection types:
- **person ↔ topic**: conversation pages link to topics discussed with that person
- **course ↔ topic**: academic material links to personal exploration of same concepts
- **topic ↔ topic**: thematic overlap between different knowledge areas
- **experience ↔ reflection**: what happened links to what was learned from it

Cross-section connections are almost always more valuable than within-section.

## Quality rules (anti-spam)

**MUST:**
- Pass the test: "would a reader learn something by following this link?"
- Reference specific shared content, not just shared keywords

**SHOULD:**
- Every page should have at least 2 outbound inline links (prevents orphans)
- Inline links are uncapped but governed by "first mention, don't over-link"

**MUST NOT:**
- Link based solely on keyword overlap without semantic relationship
- Create a dedicated "connections" or "see also" section — if it's not in the prose, it's not a connection
- Link every page in the same category to each other

## Lint-based link discovery

Automated agent pass for existing wikis that lack inline links:
1. Build alias map: page slug → matching text patterns (full name, course code, key phrase)
2. Scan prose for mentions of other pages
3. Replace first occurrence with `[[slug|matched text]]`
4. Conservative: skip ambiguous matches
5. Flag pages with 0-1 outbound links for review

## Renderer support

- **Inline links**: render `[[slug|Display Text]]` as clickable links showing only display text
- **Backlinks panel**: auto-generated list of pages that link TO this page — this replaces any need for a "connections" section
- Optional: mini knowledge graph showing 1-hop connections from current page

title: WikiHub PKM client app (desktop + mobile)
description:
## Vision

Build a dedicated client app for WikiHub that turns it into a full personal knowledge management (PKM) system — like Obsidian but backed by WikiHub's hosted git repos.

## Platforms

- Desktop: Electron app (or native Swift for Mac)
- Mobile: iOS / Android
- Web: could be the first step (online editor/reader)

## Key Questions to Decide

1. **Build from scratch vs. extend existing?**
   - Build from scratch — most control, cleanest WikiHub integration, but most work
   - Fork/extend an open-source markdown editor (e.g., Marktext, Nota, Obsidian-like)
   - Integrate with Notestream (IdeaFlow's existing app)
   - Wrap the WikiHub web UI in Electron as a starting point

2. **Offline-first architecture?**
   - Local git clone of wiki repos + sync on connect
   - Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins? merge?)
   - How much works without internet?

3. **What differentiates this from Obsidian?**
   - WikiHub-native: publish, fork, star, discover — social PKM
   - Agent-native: MCP server means your AI assistant can maintain your wiki
   - Karpathy pattern built in: raw/, wiki/, schema structure
   - No plugin ecosystem needed — opinionated defaults

4. **MVP scope**
   - Online web editor might be the fastest first step
   - WikiHub already renders markdown — adding an edit mode could be v0.1
   - Desktop app (Electron) as v0.2
   - Mobile as v0.3

## Why

Using WikiHub as a PKM system requires a better editing experience than the current web form. A dedicated client would make it viable for daily use — quick capture, wikilink navigation, offline editing, keyboard shortcuts, graph view, etc.

title: Wiki index pages rendered like Quartz — folder overview with page previews
description:
When you visit a wiki root or folder, the index page could show previews/excerpts of child pages — similar to how Quartz renders folder pages with cards showing page titles, excerpts, and metadata.

Currently wiki index pages are either a rendered index.md or a plain file listing. A Quartz-style index would automatically generate a richer overview with:
- Page title + first paragraph excerpt
- Last updated date
- Tags if present
- Maybe a card layout

This could be opt-in via frontmatter (layout: quartz-index) or the default when no index.md exists.

title: Make folder-first organization the default path in UX and docs
description:
## Motivation

WikiHub supports nested paths (`folder/page.md`) but doesn't surface folder-first organization as the default anywhere. Result: agents and users over-create wikis when a folder would do.

Paired with Noos philosophical reference: will link once created.

## Action items

- [ ] AGENTS.md: add a "when to create a new wiki vs. folder vs. page" heuristic. Default: page. Reach for folder when a concept accretes multiple pages. Reach for a new wiki only when the content has its own lifecycle (forkable, separate ACL, distinct identity).
- [ ] `/llms.txt` and `/AGENTS.md` route: echo the heuristic so connected agents internalize it on first contact.
- [ ] Landing page / create-wiki UX: copy nudge — "If you're adding a section to existing content, consider creating a page or folder first."
- [ ] Create-page UX: autocomplete existing folder paths; show a small tree of where you are.
- [ ] Verify page tree sidebar renders cleanly with 2–3 levels of nesting.

## Related

- wikihub-aihm — the original over-creation observation
- Concrete mistake: /@jacobcole/asks (pending migration decision)

title: Reconsider: WikiHub CLI (Obsidian shipped official CLI Feb 2026)
description:
Earlier conclusion (2026-04-20) was 'probably not worth building a WikiHub CLI right now' — partly based on the claim that Obsidian had no official CLI. That's wrong: Obsidian 1.12.0 shipped an official CLI in early access Feb 10, 2026, GA in v1.12.4 on Feb 27, 2026 (100+ commands).

This reopens the question. Strongest case for a WikiHub CLI:
- Agent-facing composable surface: pipe into jq, wire into shell scripts, cron publish
- Easier to install-and-script than requiring an MCP server
- Onboarding: wikihub signup && wikihub new && wikihub publish

Wraps existing REST/MCP layer; doesn't replace git auth flow.

Obsidian CLI refs:
- https://obsidian.md/cli

title: Magic login links — exchange API key for one-time browser login URL
description:
Add endpoint that an agent (with API key) can call to generate a short-lived, one-time-use URL that logs the user into the browser without leaking the API key.

Flow:
1. Agent: POST /api/v1/magic-link (Bearer API key) → returns {url, expires_at}
2. User clicks URL (e.g. https://wikihub.globalbr.ai/auth/magic/<token>)
3. Server validates token, sets session cookie, redirects to / (or ?next=)
4. Token marked used_at; cannot be reused

Design:
- New MagicLink model: id, user_id, token_hash (SHA-256), expires_at, used_at, created_at
- Token: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) → only hash stored in DB
- TTL: 10 minutes default, overrideable via ttl_seconds param (max 60 min)
- On consume: atomic check+mark used_at; constant-time compare
- Audit log entry on both creation and consumption
- Rate limit creation (e.g. 10/hour per user) — MVP can skip
- Update all agent surfaces (AGENTS.md, /AGENTS.md route, /llms.txt, MCP tools)
- e2e test: create link, GET /auth/magic/<token>, verify session cookie set, second GET returns 410
close_reason: Magic login was already implemented (model+endpoint+consumer+docs+test). Extended POST /api/v1/auth/magic-link to accept {username,password} in addition to Bearer so Claude can produce a login link from just a password. Added e2e test.

title: Build Karpathy import tool: auto-generate log.md + schema.md from imported wikis
description:
When importing external content (blogs, Google Sites, GitHub Pages) into WikiHub, automatically generate:

1. **log.md** — retrospective timeline reconstructed from dates in filenames/content. Each dated entry gets a log line: '## 2017-02-01 — Added: Ways to Discover Contrarian Truths'. This creates the Karpathy activity feed pattern even for imported content.

2. **schema.md** — auto-generated from analyzing the content structure. Describes what the wiki covers, how it's organized, and instructions for an agent maintaining it.

3. **Ripple updates** — when a new raw source is ingested, the agent should update relevant wiki/ compiled pages AND append to log.md.

The key Karpathy mechanic: 'Ingesting one source can update 10-15 wiki pages.' The schema tells the agent HOW to do this. log.md records THAT it happened.

This should be a reusable tool/skill, not one-off code. Every wiki import should produce this structure automatically.

Reference: Karpathy gist https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f
See also: memory/reference_llmwiki.md for the three-layer pattern.

title: private wikis leak page existence and index content to unauthenticated users
description:
Two related access control issues with private wikis:

1. **Index page visible on private wikis** — if a wiki is fully private, visiting the wiki index still renders the page. It should deny access entirely (redirect to login or show a 'no access' message).

2. **Direct links to private pages show empty page shell** — if you have a direct link to a private page (e.g. from a search engine or shared URL), the route renders the page template with no content instead of showing a clear 'no access' or 'this page is private' message. This leaks that the page exists and looks like a bug to the user.

Expected behavior:
- Private wiki index: 403/404 or redirect, not a rendered page
- Direct link to private page: clear 'no access' message, not an empty page shell

title: Orchestrator skill for tmux session management
Hover previews: show stub cards + close secondary on reentry to primary
description:
## Problem 1: Stubs flash 'Loading…' then vanish

Wikilinks to non-existent pages have class \`wikilink-broken\` (set by app/renderer.py:321). When hovered, the current code (app/templates/base.html:599) calls the API which returns 404 and the card silently disappears. User expects a dedicated stub card saying the page does not exist yet (and ideally linking to create it).

## Problem 2: Secondary card won't close when returning to primary

Current mouseleave handler in base.html:541-551 keeps a card alive if relatedTarget is any card in the stack. If user hovers card B (depth 1) then moves back to card A (depth 0), card B stays open because A is also in the stack. User expects: leaving card B (even to return to A) closes B.

## Fix plan

1. In the mouseover handler, detect \`.wikilink-broken\` links and open a stub-style card (no fetch, includes 'Stub — page not created' message).
2. Rework the card mouseleave handler to compare depths: if moving to a card at depth d' < my depth, run closeFromDepth(d' + 1). Only keep alive when moving to a deeper (or equal-depth) card.

## Scope

- app/templates/base.html only (lines ~398-653)
- Existing CSS at lines 243-261 can gain a \`.hover-preview.stub\` modifier for visual distinction
- Ties into wikihub-zmzk (broader stub styling)
